summary: I was born in Kansas City, Missouri, at Saint Luke's Hospital where my grandfather worked as an insurance salesman. My family had strong ties to the area, with my mother’s relatives and my father’s education connected to the region. Later, after living in Wyoming, we moved back to Missouri, and I attended school in Carrollton, which is near Kansas City. The city and its surroundings have been a significant part of my family’s history and my upbringing.

My mother, Anne Lee Trotter, was born in Miami Station, Missouri. That’s where her parents lived when I was born, and it was part of my family’s roots in Missouri. Though I wasn’t born there myself, Miami Station is important as the place where my maternal family originated. It connects to the broader Missouri background that shaped my early life.

Saint Luke's Hospital in Kansas City is where I was born, and it also played a role in my grandfather’s career as an insurance salesman. We lived with my grandparents in Kansas City while my father was overseas during World War II. So Saint Luke's was not just a hospital but part of the family’s life during a challenging time. It’s a place tied to my earliest memories and family support.

During World War II, my father, George Weymouth Bailey, served overseas and was part of an expense-paid tour of the Mediterranean with General Patton. While he was away, my family lived with my grandparents in Kansas City. The war shaped much of our family’s movements and my father’s career path afterward. It was a defining event that influenced where we lived and how we grew up.

My father, George Weymouth Bailey, was a dedicated family dentist who grew up in Sheridan, Wyoming. He attended William Jewell College in Missouri, as did many of my mother’s relatives, which is how my parents met. After serving in World War II and touring with General Patton, he established a dental practice in Kemmerer, Wyoming, before moving back to Missouri. He was a hardworking man with a fixed routine, always on call for his patients, and deeply respected in his community until his tragic death at Shangri-La Marina.

Sheridan, Wyoming, is where my father grew up along with his siblings. It’s a place that connects to his early life before he went to William Jewell College in Missouri. Though I didn’t live there myself, Sheridan was important in shaping my father’s background and values. It represents the Wyoming roots on my father’s side of the family.

William Jewell College in Liberty, Missouri, was where my father and his siblings went to school, as well as several of my mother’s relatives. It’s a small college near Kansas City that brought my parents’ families together. My brother and I also had connections to the college through family and friends, making it a significant educational institution in our family history.

After World War II, my family moved to Kemmerer, Wyoming, where my father started his dental practice as the only dentist for miles around. I attended my first school there, and it was a small town with a close-knit community. We lived there until my mother’s mother developed rheumatoid arthritis, which prompted us to move back to Missouri. Kemmerer represents my early childhood and my father’s professional beginnings.

During World War II, my father was part of an expense-paid tour of the Mediterranean Sea with General Patton, traveling from North Africa to Sicily and then Italy. This experience was part of his military service before he returned to civilian life. The Mediterranean was a backdrop for his wartime journey and a significant chapter in his life story.

General George S. Patton was the commander under whom my father served during World War II. My father had an expense-paid tour with Patton’s forces through the Mediterranean, including North Africa, Sicily, and Italy. Patton’s leadership was a key part of my father’s military experience, which influenced his life after the war.

My father participated in the North Africa Campaign during World War II as part of General Patton’s forces. This campaign was the beginning of his Mediterranean tour before moving on to Sicily and Italy. It was a significant military event that shaped his wartime service and my family’s history.

The Sicily Campaign was part of my father’s military service during World War II with General Patton’s forces. After North Africa, he moved through Sicily as part of the Mediterranean tour. This campaign was a key stage in the war and in my father’s experience before returning home to start his dental practice.

Italy was the final part of my father’s World War II Mediterranean tour with General Patton. After campaigns in North Africa and Sicily, he served in Italy before coming back to civilian life. Italy marks the conclusion of his wartime journey and the transition to his post-war career.

I spent a lot of time involved in the United Way, which reflects my commitment to community and helping others. The organization’s focus on supporting families, neighbors, and churches resonates with my experience growing up in small towns. United Way embodies the spirit of community care that I value deeply.

I attended junior high and high school in Carrollton, Missouri, a farming community located on the Missouri River bluff east of Kansas City. It was there that I was active in sports like football, basketball, and baseball. Carrollton was an important place for my adolescent years and helped shape my early interests and education.

Carrollton, Missouri, where I went to school, is situated on the Missouri River bluff. The river and its surroundings were part of the landscape of my youth in Missouri. It’s a geographic feature tied to my schooling and the community I grew up in after moving back from Wyoming.

A contemporary of mine, Jim, who petitioned with me to add an algebra class in high school, went on to become president of the University of Michigan as a nuclear physicist. While I didn’t attend there myself, the University of Michigan represents the academic aspirations and achievements of people close to me. It’s part of the broader educational context of my youth.

Jim, a contemporary of mine from high school who was involved in pushing for advanced math classes, later worked at the Williams Companies after becoming a nuclear physicist and president of the University of Michigan. Though I wasn’t directly connected to Williams Companies, it’s linked to the success of people I knew growing up. It symbolizes the career paths that stemmed from our early education.

Shangri-La Marina is where my father tragically died in a car accident about 25 years ago. He missed a turn at the marina, and his car went into the water on a dark, rainy night. Despite efforts to rescue him, he drowned, which was a devastating loss for our family. The marina later added fencing to prevent such accidents, and it remains a poignant place in our family history.

After my father’s accident at Shangri-La Marina, he was taken to a hospital in Grove, Oklahoma. That’s where we received the call about his condition, and the hospital staff were very gracious and supportive during that difficult time. Grove became part of the story of my father’s final moments and our family’s response to the tragedy.
